elements.sent-2411-001: We prove that for every compact, convex subset $K\subset\mathbb{R ^2$ the operator system $A(K)$, consisting of all continuous affine functions on $K$, is hyperrigid in the C*-algebra $C(ex(K))$.}
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, this result implies that the weak and strong operator topologies coincide on the set \begin{equation}\{T\in\mathcal{B}(H);\ T\ normal\ and\ \sigma(T)\subsetex(K)\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our approach relies on geometric properties of $K$ and generalizes previous results by Brown.
elements.sent-2411-004: The author was partially supported by the Emmy Noether Program of the German Research Foundation (DFG Grant 466012782).
elements.sent-2411-005: An operator system $S$ that generates a C*-algebra $C^*(S)$ is called hyperrigid if for every unital *-homomorphism $\pi:C^*(S)\to\mathcal{B (H)$ has the unique extension property, that is, the only unital completely positive map $\phi:C^*(S)\to\mathcal{B (H)$ satisfying $\pi|_S=\phi|_S$ is $\pi$ itself.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: In 2011, Arveson conjectured in \cite{WA that $S$ is hyperrigid if and only if the restrictions of all irreducible representations have the unique extension property.}
elements.sent-2411-007: This problem continues to attract significant interest, see \cite{RC , \cite{CT , \cite{CS and \cite{BB .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Although the conjecture has recently been disproven in \cite{BD , its validity for operator systems in commutative C*-algebras remains unsolved, for partial results see \cite{DK , \cite{PS and \cite{WA .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Among the contributions to the hyperrigidity conjecture, one noteworthy yet less well-known paper is Lawrence Brown's \cite{LB , which shows that the operator systems $\langle 1,t,f\rangle$ are hyperrigid in $C([0,1])$ for strictly convex/concave $f$.}
elements.sent-2411-010: There are already some results inspired by this, \cite{PS , but we are going in a completely different direction.}
elements.sent-2411-011: In this paper we show the following main \Cref{ThmMain .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The basic idea is similar to Brown's, but there are crucial differences.
elements.sent-2411-013: We cannot directly use left and right derivatives of functions in $A(K)$, instead we show in \Cref{Section2 that the polar parametrization of $K$ has left and right derivatives and use this to construct certain hyperplanes.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Afterwards, we examine the function measuring the angles between these hyperplanes and collect several lemmas needed to prove the main theorem.
elements.sent-2411-015: At the beginning of \Cref{Section3 , we explain our approach in detail.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Roughly speaking, we use a composition of a translation and rotation to separate connected disjoint subsets of $\partial K$.
elements.sent-2411-017: This leads us to two values which are given by basic geometric properties of $K$ and are studied in \Cref{Section3.1 respectively \Cref{Section3.2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Afterwards we are finally ready to prove \Cref{ThmMain and the following corollary in \Cref{Section3.3 .}}

elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{Fig4.png} \caption{$\partial\mathbb{D}$ with $\alpha=9/8\pi$, $a=11/8\pi$, $b=13/8\pi$ and $\beta=15/8\pi$. The hyperplanes $\partial^+_{p(\alpha)}, \partial^-_{p(\beta)}$ and $[p(\alpha):p(\beta)]$ are solid, $\partial^+_{p(a)}, \partial^-_{p(b)}$ and $[p(a):p(b)]$ are dashed. The set $p(J)$ is given by the dotted line.} \label{Fig4}
elements.sent-2411-109: The following lemma and its proof are visualized in \Cref{Fig4 .}
elements.sent-2411-110: The lemma is geometrically intuitive, but the actual mathematical proof convoluted.
elements.sent-2411-111: In the proof, we will also use the following observation, which the reader should know before reading the proof.
elements.sent-2411-112: If we have $\alpha<\beta\in[0,2\pi)$ and a supporting hyperplane containing $p(\alpha)$ and $p(\beta)$, then either $p([\alpha,\beta])$ or $p([0,2\pi)\setminus(\alpha,\beta))$ are contained in a face of $K$.
elements.sent-2411-113: It seems useless to rule out this almost trivial observation, however, we have to get rid of this case later.
elements.sent-2411-114: So let $p([0,2\pi)\setminus(\alpha,\beta))$ not be contained in $[p(\alpha):p(\beta)]$.
elements.sent-2411-115: The following is a general construction for which \Cref{Fig4 is helpful for understanding.}
elements.sent-2411-116: The hyperplanes $\partial^+_{p(\alpha)}$ and $\partial^-_{p(\beta)}$ divide $\mathbb{R ^2$ into up to four convex sets, one of which contains $K$ and is given by \Cref{RemLeft by \begin{equation}H=\{z\in\mathbb{R}^2;\ \langle z-p(\beta),ip'_-(\beta)\rangle\ge0,\ \langle z-p(\alpha),ip'_+(\alpha)\rangle\ge0\}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: Furthermore, the hyperplane $[p(\alpha):p(\beta)]$ splits $H$ into two further closed convex sets \begin{equation}\begin{split} H_1=H\cap\{z\in\mathbb{R}^2;\ \langle z-p(\alpha),i(p(\beta)-p(\alpha))\rangle\ge0\}, H_2=H\cap\{z\in\mathbb{R}^2;\ \langle z-p(\alpha),i(p(\beta)-p(\alpha))\rangle\le0\}. \end{split}\end{equation} Similarly to \Cref{RemLeft , one sees that $p(J)\subset H_1$ and $p([\alpha,\beta])\subset H_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Let us quickly argue why it suffices to show that \begin{equation}(\partial^+_{p(a)}\cup\partial^-_{p(b)}\cup[p(a):p(b)])\cap Int(H_1)=\emptyset. \label{EqHypDist}\end{equation} Assume that we have shown \Cref{EqHypDist and that the lemma is false.}

elements.sent-2411-120: However, $\partial H_1\subset \partial^+_{p(\alpha)}\cup\partial^-_{p(\beta)}\cup[p(\alpha):p(\beta)]$, from which it follows that $z\notin H_1$ and therefore we either have \begin{equation}\langle z-p(\beta),ip'_-(\beta)\rangle<0\end{equation} or \begin{equation}\langle z-p(\alpha),ip'_+(\alpha)\rangle<0\end{equation} or \begin{equation}\langle z-p(\alpha),i(p(\beta)-p(\alpha))<0.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-124: By construction we have that \begin{equation}\begin{split} \langle z-p(\alpha), i(p(\beta)-p(\alpha))\rangle>0\ and \langle z-p(\alpha), ip'_+(\alpha)\rangle>0\ and \langle z-p(\beta), ip'_-(\beta)\rangle>0, \end{split}\end{equation} since $z\inInt(H_1)$, and since $p([a,b])\subset p([\alpha,\beta])\subset H_2$, we also have that \begin{equation}\begin{split} \langle p(a)-p(\alpha),i(p(\beta)-p(\alpha))\rangle\le0\ and \langle p(b)-p(\alpha),i(p(\beta)-p(\alpha))\rangle\le0. \end{split}\end{equation} Thus there is a $w\in\{tz+(1-t)p(a);\ t\in[0,1]\}$ such that \begin{equation}\langle \omega-p(\alpha),i(p(\beta)-p(\alpha))\rangle=0.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-125: We also know that $w\in H\cap[p(\alpha):p(\beta)]$, since $z$ and $p(a)$ are in $H$,.

elements.sent-2411-133: If $z\in\partial^+_{p(a)}$, then, because of $z\inInt(H_1)$, $\partial^+_{p(a)}\neq[p(\alpha):p(\beta)]$ and $w\in\{tz+(1-t)p(a);\ t\in[0,1]\}\subset\partial^+_{p(a)}$, implying that \begin{equation}\langle w-p(a),ip'_+(a)\rangle=0\end{equation} and either \begin{equation}\langle p(\alpha)-p(a),ip'_+(a)\rangle>0\end{equation} or \begin{equation}\langle p(\beta)-p(a),ip'_+(a)\rangle>0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-134: However, the equation \begin{equation}0=\langle w-p(a),ip'_+(a)\rangle=s\langle p(\alpha)-p(a),ip'_+(a)\rangle+(1-s)\langle p(\beta)-p(a),ip'_+(a)\rangle\end{equation} shows that $p(\alpha)$ and $p(\beta)$ are not contained in the same half space bounded by $\partial^+_{p(a)}$, contradicting the fact that $\partial^+_{p(a)}$ is a supporting hyperplane.
elements.sent-2411-135: The case $z\in\partial^-_{p(b)}$ is treated analogously by considering $\tilde \omega\in\{tz+(1-t)p(b);\ t\in[0,1]\}$ with \begin{equation}\langle \tilde\omega-p(\alpha),i(p(\alpha)-p(\beta))\rangle=0\end{equation} and doing the above with $\tilde\omega$ instead of $\omega$.
elements.sent-2411-137: Similarly to \Cref{RemLeft , we see that \begin{equation}\begin{split} \langle p(\alpha)-p(a),i(p(b)-p(a))\rangle\ge0, \langle p(\beta)-p(a),i(p(b)-p(a))\rangle\ge0. \end{split}\end{equation} Thus, \begin{equation}\begin{split} 0 =\langle\omega-p(a),i(p(b)-p(a))\rangle =s\langle p(\alpha)-p(a),i(p(b)-p(a))\rangle +(1-s)\langle p(\beta)-p(a),i(p(b)-p(a))\rangle \end{split}\end{equation} implies that $p(\alpha), p(\beta)\in[p(a):p(b)]$ and hence $[p(a):p(b)]=[p(\alpha):p(\beta)]$, contradicting $z\inInt(H_1)$.. $ \square$.}
